How lighthouses standardized their signals

Before 1820, every lighthouse flashed on its own schedule. Captains kept private notebooks matching towers to patterns, and the notebooks disagreed.

The fix was boring and brilliant: publish one national table of light characteristics, and require every keeper to match it exactly.

A signal only works when the reader and the writer share the same table. The table is the product. The tower is the wrapper.

Standards feel like bureaucracy until the night you are the captain reading the wrong notebook.
